steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The second team predicts that in low-visibility conditions, wider-headed fish will be less likely than narrower-headed fish to detect obstructions. The correct answer must show this head-width comparison under low-visibility conditions, with wide-headed fish performing worse.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘A study using obstructions that created medium-sized vortices in low-visibility conditions found that some specimens of dusky smooth-hound (*Mustelus canis*), which has a relatively narrow head, bumped into the obstructions more often than other specimens of the same fish did.’: does not compare wide-headed and narrow-headed fish. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘A study using obstructions that created medium-sized vortices in low-visibility conditions found that the wider-headed bristlemouth (*Chaetostoma yurubiense*) bumped into obstructions more often than the narrower-headed dusky smooth-hound (*Mustelus canis*) did.’: wide-headed bristlemouth bumped into more obstructions than narrow-headed smooth-hound in medium-vortex, low-visibility conditions — directly matches the prediction. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘A study using obstructions that created medium-sized vortices in low-visibility conditions found that the narrower-headed dusky smooth-hound (*Mustelus canis*) bumped into the obstructions just as often as the wider-headed bristlemouth (*Chaetostoma yurubiense*) did.’: shows no difference between head types. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘A study using obstructions that created medium-sized vortices in low-visibility conditions found that the bristlemouth (*Chaetostoma yurubiense*), which has a relatively wide head, bumped into more than half of the obstructions.’: lacks the required comparison or uses wrong conditions. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
